title: Input with Scanner
subtitle: Let your programs listen — ask a question and read what the user types back.
goal: Use java.util.Scanner to read text and numbers typed by the user, then build small interactive programs like a greeter and a calculator.

blocks.heading: Programs that listen 👂
blocks.html:
So far your programs only talk — they print things. Today they learn to listen. When a program can read what you type on the keyboard, it becomes interactive: it can ask your name, ask two numbers, ask anything!

In Java, the helper that reads keyboard input is called Scanner. Think of it as a tiny robot that waits for you to type and press Enter, then hands your words to the program.

blocks.heading: Step 1: import Scanner
blocks.html:
Scanner lives in a toolbox called java.util. To use it, you tell Java to bring it in at the very top of your file:

import java.util.Scanner;

Then you create one connected to the keyboard (System.in means "the keyboard"):

Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in);

blocks.caption: A greeter that reads your name
blocks.explain: sc.nextLine() reads one whole line of text that you type. If you type Sam and press Enter, the program prints: Hello, Sam!

blocks.html: Why no Run button works here? These programs wait for you to type. Our checker can't type for them, so we just read the code instead of running it. On your own computer they run perfectly — try them!

blocks.heading: Reading numbers: nextInt and nextDouble
blocks.html:
nextLine() reads text. But Scanner can also read numbers:

  • nextInt() reads a whole number, like 7
  • nextDouble() reads a number with a decimal point, like 3.5

Once you read a number you can do math with it — add, subtract, multiply, anything!

blocks.caption: A simple calculator: add two numbers
blocks.explain: nextInt() reads a whole number. If you type 4 then 5, the program prints: The sum is 9

blocks.caption: Polishing output with String.format
blocks.explain: String.format builds a tidy sentence. %s drops in the name, %.2f shows the price with 2 decimals. If you type Mia then 3.5, it prints: Mia, that costs $3.50

blocks.caption: A runnable peek (no typing needed) — same idea with a fixed name
blocks.output: Hello, Sam! You are 11 years old.
blocks.explain: This one has no Scanner, so it runs! It shows what String.format does: %s becomes the name, %d becomes the whole number. Press Run to see it work.

blocks.html: Watch the order! Number readers like nextInt() leave behind the leftover Enter key. If you call nextLine() right after, it can grab that empty leftover. Pro tip: when mixing, add an extra sc.nextLine(); after a number read to clear it.

blocks.question: Which line creates a Scanner that reads from the keyboard?
blocks.explain: new Scanner(System.in) makes a Scanner connected to System.in, which is the keyboard.

blocks.question: You want to read a number like 3.5 that has a decimal point. Which method do you use?
blocks.explain: nextDouble() reads numbers with decimals. nextInt() is only for whole numbers, and nextWord() isn't a real method.

blocks.question: What MUST you put at the top of your file to use Scanner?
blocks.explain: Scanner lives in the java.util toolbox, so you bring it in with import java.util.Scanner; at the top.

blocks.title: Build a greeter
blocks.instructions: Finish the program so it reads the user's name with sc.nextLine() and then prints a greeting using that name. Your code must use Scanner and nextLine.
blocks.passMsg: Nice — your program can greet anyone who types their name!
blocks.failMsg: Make sure you use Scanner, call sc.nextLine(), and print a line containing Hello.

blocks.title: Add two numbers
blocks.instructions: Read two whole numbers with nextInt(), add them, and print the result. Your code must use Scanner and nextInt.
blocks.passMsg: You built a calculator! It adds whatever two numbers the user types.
blocks.failMsg: Use Scanner, read both numbers with nextInt(), and add them with the + sign.

challenge.title: Make an interactive mini profile
challenge.instructions: Write a program that asks the user for their name (use nextLine) and their age (use nextInt), then prints a friendly sentence using String.format that includes BOTH. Your code must use Scanner, nextLine, nextInt, and String.format.
challenge.hint: Read the name with sc.nextLine() into a String, read the age with sc.nextInt() into an int, then build the sentence with String.format("Hi %s, you are %d years old!", name, age).
challenge.passMsg: 🏆 Day 5 complete! Your programs can now listen, do math, and reply — that's real interactivity.
